title: Schizophrenia and the Book of Changes
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: Philip K. Dick's essay 'Schizophrenia and the Book of Changes,' a non-fiction philosophical piece in which Dick explores the relationship between schizophrenic experience and the I Ching (Book of Changes). The essay reflects Dick's lifelong engagement with Eastern philosophy, altered states of consciousness, and the nature of reality. One of several non-fiction essays Dick wrote examining the boundaries between mental illness, perception, and metaphysical insight.
page_summary:
A 22-page scanned document of Philip K. Dick's essay 'Schizophrenia and the Book of Changes,' a known non-fiction philosophical piece. No OCR text layer is present. In this essay, Dick explores the relationship between schizophrenic perception and the divinatory system of the I Ching (Book of Changes), the ancient Chinese oracle that played a significant role in his creative process.

Dick famously used the I Ching to plot The Man in the High Castle (1962), consulting the oracle to determine narrative decisions, and the Book of Changes appears as a central element within the novel itself. This essay represents Dick's theoretical engagement with the I Ching's implications for understanding consciousness, reality, and the nature of meaningful patterns. The connection between schizophrenia and the I Ching reflects Dick's lifelong interest in altered states of consciousness as potential modes of perceiving hidden structures of reality.
